key_insight: Interval and fuzzy physics-augmented neural networks learn physics-consistent hyperelastic energy bounds that enclose uncertain stress data and quantify uncertainty without assuming a probability distribution.
engineering_relevance: The framework offers a compact, mechanics-informed way to build constitutive models that remain reliable under sparse, noisy, or heterogeneous data and can propagate uncertainty into finite element simulations.
method: Train sparse mean, lower, and upper free-energy branches with automatic differentiation to generate stress bounds.
Use a two-stage transfer-learning strategy: learn the mean response first, then fine-tune bound branches.
Embed mechanistic constraints and smooth L0 regularization to promote objectivity, consistency, polyconvexity, and interpretability.
evidence_signal: Evaluated on synthetic isotropic hyperelastic datasets with heteroscedastic, shifted-mean, and varying-magnitude noise, where the learned bounds enclose noisy stress observations and generalize to test data.
watch_item: Performance depends on the quality of the sparse training data and the transfer-learning setup, so poorly chosen regularization or bound initialization could lead to overly conservative or loose envelopes.
paper_title: Interval and fuzzy physics-augmented neural networks (iPANN and fPANN) for uncertainty quantification and propagation in constitutive modeling
